qa.a: Contrast-induced nephropathy, the acute kidney injury that can follow the administration of iodinated radiographic contrast media, is a recognised cause of renal injury in patients undergoing investigations and procedures, and its understanding allows its prevention in those at risk. It is an acute deterioration in renal function occurring within a few days of receiving iodinated contrast, attributed to a combination of mechanisms: a direct toxic effect of the contrast on the tubular cells, and renal vasoconstriction reducing the medullary blood flow and causing medullary ischaemia, to which, as we have noted, the medulla is vulnerable. The risk is greatest in those with pre-existing renal impairment, particularly diabetic nephropathy, and in the volume-depleted, and rises with the dose of contrast. Its prevention, in those at risk, rests chiefly on ensuring an adequate hydration and circulating volume around the time of the contrast, which maintains the renal and medullary perfusion and dilutes the contrast — intravenous fluid being the mainstay; on minimising the dose of contrast and using lower-toxicity agents; and on avoiding other nephrotoxins and, where possible, withholding drugs such as the non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs around the time. So contrast-induced nephropathy is an acute kidney injury after iodinated contrast, from direct tubular toxicity and medullary ischaemic vasoconstriction, the risk greatest in pre-existing renal impairment and volume depletion; prevented chiefly by adequate hydration to maintain perfusion, by minimising the contrast dose, and by avoiding other nephrotoxins — a preventable cause of renal injury in those undergoing contrast studies.

qa.q: Describe the hepatorenal syndrome.
qa.a: The hepatorenal syndrome is a particular form of renal failure that occurs in advanced liver disease, in which the kidneys fail despite being structurally normal, and it is an instructive and serious condition illustrating the dependence of renal function on the systemic circulation. It occurs in patients with severe liver disease and portal hypertension, typically with cirrhosis and ascites. The mechanism is circulatory: the liver disease and portal hypertension cause a marked splanchnic and systemic vasodilatation, which reduces the effective arterial blood volume; this is sensed as a fall in the effective circulating volume, triggering an intense activation of the renin-angiotensin system, the sympathetic nervous system and antidiuretic hormone, which cause a severe renal vasoconstriction. The result is a profound reduction in renal blood flow and glomerular filtration — a functional renal failure — even though the kidneys themselves are structurally normal, as shown by the fact that they can function normally if transplanted into a recipient with a normal circulation, or if the liver disease is reversed. The urine is sodium-poor, as in pre-renal failure. The prognosis is poor without treatment of the underlying liver disease, definitively by liver transplantation. So the hepatorenal syndrome is a functional renal failure in advanced liver disease, in which splanchnic and systemic vasodilatation reduces the effective arterial volume, triggering intense renal vasoconstriction through the renin-angiotensin, sympathetic and antidiuretic-hormone systems — the structurally normal kidneys failing from the circulatory disturbance; reversible with the liver disease and definitively treated by liver transplantation, a serious illustration of the dependence of the kidney on the systemic circulation.

qa.q: Thank you. A clear account. Let us stop there. Before we close, here are the take-home messages. First, The kidney excretes metabolic wastes, regulates water/electrolyte and acid-base balance and (long-term) blood pressure, and has endocrine functions (erythropoietin, vitamin D activation, renin); the Second, The renal circulation receives a fifth to a quarter of cardiac output (~1200 mL/min), far beyond metabolic need (it serves filtration), arranged uniquely as two capillary beds in series — high-pressure glomerular (filtration) and low-pressure peritubular/vasa recta (reabsorption) — separated by the efferent arteriole; the two arterioles control glomerular pressure and filtration. Third, Flow is distributed unevenly: the cortex receives most (suited to filtration); the medulla only a few per cent — necessary to preserve its concentration gradient but leaving it on the edge of hypoxia and vulnerable to ischaemic injury. Fourth, Renal blood flow and GFR are autoregulated, held nearly constant over a mean arterial pressure of ~80-180 mmHg, by the myogenic response (afferent arteriole constricts to a rise in pressure) and tubuloglomerular feedback (macula densa). The juxtaglomerular apparatus — macula densa (senses tubular NaCl), renin-secreting JG cells (afferent arteriole, sense pressure), mesangial cells — mediates tubuloglomerular feedback and renin control. Fifth, Renal blood flow is measured by PAH clearance (almost completely cleared in one passage → clearance = renal plasma flow by the Fick principle), converted to blood flow via the haematocrit. Though autoregulated, flow is reduced by sympathetic activity and vasoconstrictors (angiotensin II, catecholamines) in stress/haemorrhage/surgery — so maintaining volume and blood pressure and avoiding nephrotoxins are central to perioperative renal protection. Thank you. That concludes the station.
